verdict: You're clearly set up as an accountant for small businesses — but visitors are leaving without getting in touch because every question they arrived with (how much does it cost, who have you helped, why you over anyone else) goes completely unanswered.
scope_note: This report looks at whether your site is set up to turn visitors into enquiries — the strategy, messaging, and content that make someone decide to get in touch. Design matters too, but judging it properly needs a conversation about your business, your clients, and your goals. That's one for a call.

fixes.title: Every 'Learn More' button skips to your booking form before visitors are ready
fixes.detail: A business owner scrolling through your services sees 'Learn More' and clicks expecting detail — instead, they're sent straight to 'Book a free 15-minute consultation.' That's asking for a commitment before they understand what they're buying. Most visitors who aren't ready to book will just leave rather than scroll back up to keep reading.
fixes.action: Update each Learn More button to expand or link to the relevant service description — a visitor who fully understands what you offer is far more likely to book a call than one who's still figuring it out.

fixes.title: There's no way to get in touch without picking up the phone or opening an email app
fixes.detail: The only contact options are a phone number and an email address — nothing someone can fill in at 10pm when they're thinking about their finances. On mobile, tapping an email address opens an app most people don't have set up, and many visitors will abandon rather than switch apps. Every extra step between 'I want to get in touch' and 'I've got in touch' costs you enquiries.
fixes.action: Add a Calendly link so someone can book a 15-minute call directly from the site without needing their phone or email app — it takes under an hour to set up and immediately removes the biggest friction point.

fixes.title: There's no mention of cost anywhere — so visitors assume the worst and leave
fixes.detail: A small business owner weighing up whether they can afford an accountant will look for a price before they do anything else. When they can't find one, they don't call to ask — they assume expensive and move on to someone willing to give them a ballpark. You don't need a full price list; you just need enough for the right people to decide it's worth getting in touch.
fixes.action: Add one sentence explaining how you charge — even 'monthly retainers for small businesses typically start at £X' removes the biggest reason people leave without making contact.

fixes.title: There's nothing online to confirm Jack is real and trustworthy beyond the site itself
fixes.detail: A business owner who's interested will Google Jack before they call. Right now there's nothing from anyone other than Jack saying he's worth hiring — no Google reviews, no independent listings, nothing. The three testimonials on the site have no names or photos, which makes them nearly impossible for a stranger to take seriously.
fixes.action: Set up a Google Business Profile and ask your two or three best clients to leave a review — even five real Google reviews transforms how you appear to someone comparing accountants in Chester-le-Street.

fixes.title: Visitors don't know who they'd actually be working with
fixes.detail: There's no section introducing Jack — no photo on the homepage, no story, and the AAT qualification that appears in the browser tab description isn't visible anywhere on the page itself. For a service this personal — someone with full access to your finances — people need to feel they know something about who they're calling before they'll pick up the phone.
fixes.action: Add a short section with a real photo, two or three sentences about your background and why you work with small businesses, and your AAT qualification clearly visible — a photo next to a short paragraph is enough.

findings.check: The site puts the client first, not the business
findings.note: There is some client-focused language — 'letting you focus on what you do best', 'you're running a small business and need practical help' — but most major sections open with 'At Sinclair Accounting, we...' which puts the business at the centre. A visitor landing here doesn't immediately feel 'this site gets me'; they feel 'this site is about them.'
findings.rec: Rewrite section openers so they start with the client's situation — 'You're running a business and tax is the last thing you want to think about' opens in their world, not yours.

findings.check: The core problem the client faces is named clearly
findings.note: The site mentions 'stress of taxes' and 'simplifying financial information' — the problem is present but surface-level. The specific, felt fear that actually drives a small business owner to find an accountant — an unexpected tax bill, cash flow drying up, getting it wrong and facing penalties — isn't named anywhere.
findings.rec: Name one concrete problem in the opening section — 'Not knowing what you owe until HMRC tells you' is something your ideal client actually worries about.

findings.check: Specialist, not generalist
findings.note: 'Accountants for small businesses' describes almost every accountant in the country. The tax planning section mentions 'service businesses' which hints at a niche, but it's buried and inconsistent — there's no clear signal of who you're best at helping.
findings.rec: Decide on the type of small business you most want to work with and say so upfront — 'service businesses in the North East' is a far stronger position than 'small businesses'.

findings.check: Offer clarity
findings.note: The three service tiers are named but never explained in enough detail for a visitor to understand what they'd actually get — and all the 'Learn More' links skip straight to the booking form rather than providing any further information. A visitor can't work out whether this is the right fit without picking up the phone.
findings.rec: Add at least two or three sentences per service tier explaining what's included and who it's designed for, so visitors can self-qualify before making contact.

findings.check: Main call to action AND a lower-commitment option both present
findings.note: The only way to take action is to call or email — there's no lower-commitment option for someone who's interested but not quite ready to pick up the phone. Visitors who aren't ready to act yet have no reason to stay connected.
findings.rec: Add one lower-commitment option — a free guide, a short email series, or a 'subscribe for tax tips' — so the people who aren't ready to call yet have a reason to stay in touch.

findings.check: Reflects current business
findings.note: The service content appears current, but the 'hear the latest news' section contains no articles — just a heading and two images. An empty content section signals the site was set up at launch and hasn't been touched since.
findings.rec: Either publish one real article or remove the news section — an empty section is worse than no section at all.

findings.check: Clear differentiator
findings.note: Remove the name and logo — this site could belong to any small business accountant in the UK. 'Your Partner in Financial Growth', 'stress-free accounts', 'tailored levels of service' are phrases every accountant uses, and none of them give a visitor a reason to choose Jack over anyone else.
findings.rec: Identify one thing only you can say — your background, a specific client result, the type of business you understand deeply — and make it the central message.

findings.check: Opening headline connects to the client's desire or situation
findings.note: 'Accountants for small businesses' is a category label, and 'Your Partner in Financial Growth' describes the business relationship rather than what a client actually wants. Neither makes the right person think 'that's exactly me' or pulls them toward the outcome they're after — more money in their pocket, less time worrying about tax, a business they feel in control of.
findings.rec: Rewrite the headline to speak to the desire your ideal client has — something like 'Run your business. We'll make sure HMRC isn't a problem.' gives them a reason to keep reading.

findings.check: The emotional frustration behind the practical problem is addressed
findings.note: The site mentions 'stress of taxes' but never goes deeper into what that stress feels like — the anxiety every time an unexpected bill arrives, the feeling of being out of control of your own money, the worry that you might be getting it wrong. Without naming the feeling, the site stays on the surface of why someone actually picks up the phone.
findings.rec: Add one sentence that shows you understand the feeling underneath the problem — 'Most of our clients came to us after a tax bill they weren't expecting' does more work than any list of services.

findings.check: The bigger picture — what's really at stake — comes through
findings.note: There's no sense of what having the right accountant actually makes possible for a small business owner beyond 'financial growth' — a vague phrase that slides off the page. What does a financially sorted business actually give you? The confidence to hire? The freedom to take on more work? A business you could eventually sell?
findings.rec: Add one line about what this really makes possible — not 'growth' but the specific thing your best clients tell you their life looks like now.

findings.check: The site shows it understands the client's situation before it sells anything
findings.note: The site moves straight into listing services without first showing it understands what a small business owner is dealing with day to day. Visitors feel sold to rather than understood, and people don't buy from businesses that pitch before they connect.
findings.rec: Open with one or two sentences that describe the client's world before you mention a single service — show you get their situation before you offer to fix it.

findings.check: Credibility and expertise are clearly evidenced
findings.note: Your AAT qualification is mentioned in the browser tab description but doesn't appear anywhere on the page a visitor can actually read. There's no mention of qualifications, how long you've been doing this, or any named result — just claims without proof.
findings.rec: Add your AAT qualification and a brief note on your experience somewhere visible on the page — credentials you've earned should be working for you, not hidden in a meta tag.

findings.check: What happens next is explained clearly
findings.note: There's no explanation of the process — what happens after someone gets in touch, what the first conversation looks like, how quickly they'd hear back. A visitor who doesn't know what they're committing to when they click 'Book a call' is less likely to do it.
findings.rec: Add three simple steps — 'Book a free 15-min call → We review your situation → You get a clear plan, in plain English' — it removes the uncertainty that stops people from making contact.

findings.check: What life looks like after working with this business is described
findings.note: 'Letting you focus on what you do best' and 'unbelievable growth' in the testimonials hint at the outcome, but they're vague enough that a visitor can't picture what their business actually looks like once they're working with you.
findings.rec: Get specific: 'Close your laptop on Friday knowing exactly what you owe, what's coming in, and what you can pay yourself' gives your ideal client something real to want.

findings.check: There's a reason to act now, not later
findings.note: Nothing on the site makes the status quo feel uncomfortable — no sense of what's being lost every month without proper financial management, no urgency, no reason today is better than next month. A visitor on the fence will bookmark the page and never come back.
findings.rec: Add one honest line that makes waiting feel costly — 'Every month without a clear view of your finances is a month you might be overpaying tax or missing a growth opportunity' is specific and true.

findings.check: Clear within 5 seconds what you do and who for
findings.note: Within five seconds, a visitor can see this is an accountancy firm for small businesses in Chester-le-Street — the headline, location, and call to action are all visible immediately without scrolling.

findings.check: Headings pull the reader forward, not just label sections
findings.note: Every section heading is a label — 'Our Services', 'Management Accounts', 'Tax Planning', 'Those who know us best'. None of them give a reason to read what follows or connect to a problem the visitor is trying to solve.
findings.rec: Rewrite headings as outcomes or questions — 'Stop paying more tax than you need to' does more work than 'Tax Planning' and gives a reader a reason to keep going.

findings.check: Copy is written about the client, not the business
findings.note: The Level 1 service description opens in the client's world ('You're running a small business and need practical, hands-on help') which is strong — but most other sections open with 'At Sinclair Accounting, we...' and the ratio tips business-first overall.
findings.rec: Carry the Level 1 tone throughout — every section that opens with 'we' or 'at Sinclair' should be rewritten to open with the client's situation instead.

findings.check: Services describe what the client gets, not just what the service is called
findings.note: The service level descriptions have some useful client language, but 'Essential Support', 'Strategic Growth', and 'Financial Partnership' are labels that don't tell a visitor what they actually receive — and the Management Accounts and Tax Planning sections describe features rather than the outcome for the client.
findings.rec: End each service description with the thing the client ends up with — 'so you always know what you can afford to pay yourself and what's coming next month' turns a feature into a benefit.

findings.check: No empty claims that any competitor could also make
findings.note: 'Tailored levels of service', 'hands-on help', 'Your Partner in Financial Growth', 'stress-free' — these are the exact phrases every accountancy firm uses, and because they're unproven they register as noise.
findings.rec: Replace at least one generic claim with something specific you can back up — instead of 'hands-on help', say what that looks like in practice: 'We pick up the phone same day when something urgent comes up.'

findings.note: See Buyer Questions — pricing is covered there.
findings.check: Pricing addressed somewhere
Long copy genuinely scannable
findings.note: The page is broadly broken into visual sections which helps, but the Management Accounts paragraph is a dense block of text that a visitor reading on their phone at speed will skip entirely.
findings.rec: Break the Management Accounts and Tax Planning descriptions into shorter paragraphs or add a subheading — it should be readable in 20 seconds of scanning.

findings.check: Testimonials are specific, named, and show a real result
findings.note: All three testimonials describe real, specific outcomes — compliance relief, business growth, a penalty appeal — which gives them more weight than generic praise. None have a name, company, or photo attached though, which means a stranger has no way to verify they're real.
findings.rec: Even just adding a first name and business type to each testimonial — 'Sarah, freelance designer' — dramatically increases how much trust they carry.

findings.check: At least one case study with a real, measurable outcome
findings.note: There are no case studies anywhere on the site. The testimonials hint at good outcomes but don't explain the situation or what was done to achieve them — and that's the detail that turns a visitor into someone who believes you can solve their specific problem.
findings.rec: Write one short case study — the client's situation, what you did, and what changed — a single well-told story does more trust-building work than ten anonymous quotes.

findings.note: No logo strips or badge sections present on the site.
findings.check: Logo strips and badges show genuine proof
The About page makes the owner human AND connects to why they do this work
findings.note: There's no About section and no introduction to Jack anywhere on the homepage. For a service as personal as accounting — someone with access to your entire financial picture — visitors need to feel they know something about who they'd be working with before they'll pick up the phone.
findings.rec: Add a short section with a real photo of Jack, two or three sentences about his background, and a line on why he works with small businesses — it doesn't need to be long, it just needs to be real.

findings.check: Qualifications and credentials are visible
findings.note: The AAT qualification appears in the browser tab description but isn't visible on the page itself — a visitor reading the site won't see it. There's no other mention of qualifications, training, or professional standing anywhere.
findings.rec: Add 'AAT-qualified' and any other relevant credentials in a visible spot on the page — credentials you've earned should be doing trust-building work, not sitting in a meta tag no one reads.

findings.check: Proof sits near the point where a decision is made
findings.note: The three testimonials appear near the bottom of the page, well below where most visitors will have already decided whether to get in touch. Trust signals do their best work right next to the moment of decision.
findings.rec: Move at least one testimonial up near the opening section or alongside the main call to action — so the best evidence that you're worth hiring appears before people have clicked away.

findings.check: Cost and pricing addressed
findings.note: There's no mention of cost anywhere on the site — not a starting figure, not a pricing model, not even an indication of how you charge. A small business owner who is already cautious about accountancy fees will look for a price first, and when they can't find one, they leave rather than call to ask.
findings.rec: Add one sentence explaining how you price — even 'monthly retainers for small businesses typically start at £X' gives the right people a reason to stay and the wrong people a reason to move on.

findings.check: Honest about challenges and limitations
findings.note: The site only presents positives — there's no acknowledgment of who this isn't right for, what might be complicated, or any limitations. Businesses that only publish good news signal they're hiding something, which erodes trust.
findings.rec: Add a short line on who you work best with and who you don't — being honest about your ideal fit signals confidence, not weakness.

findings.check: Why this business over the alternatives
findings.note: The site doesn't address why a small business owner should choose Sinclair Accounting over a bigger firm, a cheaper online service, or doing it themselves with software. These are exactly the comparisons your ideal client is making — and right now a competitor is answering those questions while you aren't.
findings.rec: Add a short paragraph on what you offer that a larger firm or online service doesn't — personal availability, specific expertise, a single point of contact who actually knows your business.

findings.check: Reviews and independent proof from outside the site
findings.note: There are no independent reviews or ratings for Sinclair Accounting Services anywhere online — no Google Business Profile, no Trustpilot, no third-party listings. A prospect researching Jack before calling will find nothing from anyone other than Jack.
findings.rec: Set up a Google Business Profile and ask your best clients to leave a review — even five Google reviews transforms how you appear to someone searching for a local accountant.

findings.note: See Learning Hub — educational content is covered there.
findings.check: Useful content that teaches something without immediately selling
A visitor can work out whether this is right for them without contacting
findings.note: There's no clear description of who you help, what working together involves, or what it costs — a visitor can't self-qualify without picking up the phone. Most won't bother; they'll go to a site that tells them what they need to know.
findings.rec: Add a short 'Is this right for me?' section answering the three questions every potential client has: who do you work with, what's included, and what does it cost.

findings.check: One clear next step on every page
findings.note: The main call to action — 'Book a free 15-minute consultation' — is clear at the bottom. But the five 'Learn More' buttons that appear throughout the services section all scroll directly to the booking form rather than providing more information, which pushes visitors toward a commitment before they've had the chance to fully understand what they're buying.
findings.rec: Link the Learn More buttons to the relevant service detail rather than the booking form — let visitors educate themselves before you ask them to act.

findings.check: Homepage builds a case from start to finish
findings.note: The structure — intro, services, detailed services, testimonials, contact — is broadly logical, but the site leads with services before showing any understanding of the client's situation. A visitor who doesn't feel understood in the first two sections won't read as far as the testimonials.
findings.rec: Move the empathy before the sell — open with the client's situation, then introduce your services as the solution.

findings.check: 3 clicks or fewer from homepage to submitting an enquiry
findings.note: On a single-page site, getting in touch requires just one action — tapping the phone number or email address. The path to contact is short.

findings.check: Getting in touch is genuinely easy
findings.note: Phone and email are the only contact options — there's no form and no booking tool. On mobile, tapping an email address opens a mail app many people don't use, and there's no fallback. Visitors who want to get in touch outside of office hours or without a phone call have no good option.
findings.rec: Add a Calendly booking link or a simple contact form so someone can take action directly from the site without needing any other app.

findings.check: Navigation is set up for first-time visitors, not existing clients
findings.note: There's no page navigation — a visitor who wants to jump straight to testimonials or the contact section has to scroll the entire page to find them. On a mobile screen this is a meaningful friction point.
findings.rec: Add a simple sticky nav or anchor links to the main sections so visitors can find what they need without scrolling through everything first.

findings.check: It's clear what happens after someone gets in touch
findings.note: There's no indication of how quickly someone will hear back or what the first conversation will actually look like. A visitor who doesn't know what they're committing to when they click 'Book a call' is less likely to follow through.
findings.rec: Add a one-line response promise and a brief description of the first call — 'We'll reply within one working day; our first chat is a free 15 minutes to see if we're a good fit' removes the last reason not to get in touch.

findings.check: Content section exists with real, recent articles
findings.note: The 'hear the latest news' section contains no articles — just a heading and two images. There's nothing a visitor could read to learn something useful or build trust in Jack's expertise before making contact.
findings.rec: Publish one real article on a topic your ideal clients search for — 'How much tax should I put aside as a sole trader?' — or remove the section entirely; an empty content section is worse than no content section.

findings.note: No content exists to evaluate topics coverage.
findings.check: Content covers the topics buyers actually search for

findings.note: No content exists to evaluate navigability.
findings.check: Content is easy to find and navigate
FAQs answer the real questions buyers have before contacting
findings.note: There are no FAQs anywhere on the site. The questions a small business owner arrives with — how much does it cost, what do I actually get, how do I switch from my current accountant — all go unanswered.
findings.rec: Add a simple FAQ section with four or five real questions your potential clients actually ask — the answers are probably already in your head.

findings.check: Video content present
findings.note: There's no video on the site. A short 60-second video of Jack explaining who he helps and why he started this business would build more trust in one viewing than the entire rest of the page — people hire people, and video is the fastest way to give a stranger a sense of who you are.
findings.rec: Record a 60-second video introducing yourself and who you help — it doesn't need to be professionally produced; a phone video shot in good light is enough to start.

findings.note: No content exists to evaluate a pre-call preparation path.
findings.check: There's a path for a visitor to get ready before they make contact

findings.note: No content exists to evaluate whether it prepares prospects for a first conversation.
findings.check: Content would help a prospect feel ready for a first conversation

positives: The three testimonials describe real outcomes — tax compliance relief, a penalty appeal, and business growth — which is far more convincing than generic praise; add names and they become genuinely persuasive.

The three-tier service structure (Essential Support, Strategic Growth, Financial Partnership) is a strong signal that you grow with clients over time rather than just handling compliance — it's worth making more of in the copy.
The site loads securely and ranks first for a branded name search — the technical foundations are solid.
